ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn class for Japanese-Korean fusion bibimbap bowl with yakitori. Mapped to closest parent class 'mixed rice dish' (FOODON_00002809) as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed rice dish with meat and vegetables
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for yakitori bibimbap fusion. A0FJP (mixed rice dish with meat and vegetables) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific fusion dish preparation. SNOMED CT coverage for mixed ethnic fusion rice bowls is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, representing a full bibimbap bowl with charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori, rice, seasoned vegetables, kimchi, gochujang, and a half-boiled egg; the combo set with miso soup or drink weighs approximately 650 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods composite estimate for mixed rice dish with grilled chicken, vegetables, egg, and fermented condiments
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for this Japanese-Korean fusion dish. Per-100g values were derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for: cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 168878), grilled chicken thigh without skin (FDC ID 171477), hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424), raw spinach (FDC ID 168462), mung bean sprouts (FDC ID 169957), kimchi (FDC ID 170392), and gochujang (FDC ID 1100613). The input record's per-100g values (energy 123.64 kcal, protein 6.91 g, fat 4.0 g, carbohydrate 14.55 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 327.27 mg/100g reflects the multi-source fermented condiment load (kimchi, miso, soy sauce, gochujang) and is plausible. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, and B-vitamins are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdowns for this composite preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central at the dish level.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific fusion dish. GI of 72 is based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Gochujang and kimchi contribute minor carbohydrate fractions with lower individual GI values, but the overall dish GI is dominated by the white rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 80.0) / 100 = 57.6, rounded to 57.7, using 550 g default serving × 14.55 g carbohydrate/100g = 80.0 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High multi-source sodium load from kimchi, miso, soy sauce, gochujang, and yakitori tare sauce; sodium 327 mg/100g equates to approximately 1,800 mg per 550 g serving, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and miso, limit kimchi and gochujang portions, and avoid the miso soup add-on to keep total sodium intake within safe limits for a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with a glycemic load of approximately 57.7 per standard serving; low dietary fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio (1.09 g fibre per 14.55 g carbohydrate per 100g) provides insufficient buffering of postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a brown rice or cauliflower rice substitution to lower the glycemic index, reduce gochujang quantity to limit added sugar, and increase the proportion of spinach and bean sprouts relative to rice to improve the fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.09 g/100g and cholesterol 40 mg/100g are moderate; however, the high sodium content (327 mg/100g) and the cumulative sodium from fermented condiments pose a significant cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium and cholesterol exposure, choose grilled chicken breast over thigh to lower saturated fat, and avoid the miso soup combo option to reduce total sodium intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Kimchi provides live fermented cultures supporting gut microbiome and immune regulation; spinach and sesame oil contribute antioxidants and vitamin E; however, gochujang contains capsaicin (nightshade) which may trigger joint flare in sensitive individuals, and high sodium from fermented condiments can worsen tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory components (kimchi, spinach, sesame oil) but should monitor response to gochujang and request reduced-sodium condiments to minimise RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Yakitori Bibimbap Bowl Combo is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish sold in Singapore, combining two distinct East Asian culinary traditions in a single meal. The bowl features charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori — chicken pieces skewered and glazed with a sweet-savoury tare sauce of soy sauce, mirin, and sake — served over steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. Korean bibimbap elements are layered on top: seasoned bean sprouts (kongnamul), blanched and seasoned spinach (sigeumchi namul), fermented kimchi, and gochujang (Korean fermented chilli paste). A half-boiled egg completes the bowl, adding richness and protein. The combo set includes a choice of side and miso soup or a drink, bringing the total serving weight to approximately 650 g. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 123.6 kcal per 100g, with 6.9 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 14.6 g carbohydrate. The dominant carbohydrate source is white rice, which carries a high glycemic index (~72), making the dish a high glycemic load meal (~57.7 GL per 550 g serving). Sodium is elevated at 327 mg/100g due to the cumulative contribution of kimchi, miso, soy sauce, gochujang, and yakitori tare, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The fermented components (kimchi, miso) provide probiotic benefit and the spinach and sesame oil contribute antioxidants. This dish is not suitable for individuals with gluten intolerance due to soy sauce and miso content, and is not certified halal due to mirin (rice wine) in the yakitori glaze.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a yakitori bibimbap bowl?
Is yakitori bibimbap suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of bibimbap with yakitori?
What allergens are in yakitori bibimbap?
What is the glycemic index of bibimbap?
Can I eat yakitori bibimbap if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between yakitori and bibimbap?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Japanese-Korean fusion dish. Per-100g values are estimated from a weighted USDA FoodData Central composite of individual component foods (white rice, grilled chicken, egg, spinach, bean sprouts, kimchi, gochujang). The input record's values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (Japanese short-grain white rice, GI ~72) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level measurement from an approved primary source is available.
notes: 1. Per-100g nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central composite of component foods (SR Legacy entries for cooked short-grain white rice FDC 168878, grilled chicken thigh FDC 171477, hard-boiled egg FDC 173424, raw spinach FDC 168462, mung bean sprouts FDC 169957, kimchi FDC 170392, gochujang FDC 1100613). The input record values are consistent with this composite and are retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite fusion dish preparation are not available at the dish level in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008), which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor. 4. Two portions are listed in the input: 550 g (1 bowl) and 650 g (1 set meal with side and miso soup/drink). The 550 g bowl is used as the default serving for GL calculation. 5. The dish is not certified halal due to mirin (rice wine) in the yakitori tare glaze. 6. Input ethnic_tags included 'Beef Dish' and 'Japanese Curry' which are incorrect for this dish and have been removed. The primary cuisine is classified as Japanese-Korean Fusion, with country of origin Singapore. 7. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 8. trans_fat_g is set to 0.02 g/100g as reported in the input record, consistent with trace levels from grilled chicken and sesame oil; this is a plausible trace value and not a confirmed zero.
